coredata.dc:title: N, S co-doped carbon dots as effective corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel in CO2-saturated 3.5% NaCl solution
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Cen, Hongyu
Chen, Zhenyu
Guo, Xingpeng
coredata.dc:description: N and S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) were synthesized and used as corrosion inhibitors for the first time in this study. The inhibition performance of N, S-CDs for carbon steel in CO2-saturated NaCl solution was investigated. Results showed that N, S-CDs can effectively protect carbon steel from corrosion and the inhibition efficiency increases as the concentration of N, S-CDs increases, reaching 93% at 50 mg/L. Even at the low concentration, such as 10 mg/L N, S-CDs, the corrosion current density reduces from 1.472 × 10−4 A·cm−2 in the blank condition to 2.99 × 10−5 A·cm−2 after 12 h immersion. N, S-CDs can adsorb on carbon steel through their functional groups and nanoparticles made up of N, S-CDs can form a hydrophobic film on the metal surface, whose thickness was about 40 nm. A theoretical model is established to describe the inhibition mechanism of N, S-CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Corrosion inhibitors
Hydrophobic film
CO2 corrosion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Microstate of N, S-CDs on the metal surface

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2019 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the synthesis process of N, S-CDs.

The characterization of N, S-CDs for (a) TEM image, (b) HRTEM image, (c) XRD pattern, (d) FTIR spectra, (e) Raman spectrum (f) UV–vis absorption spectra, Inset: Pictures of the N, S-CDs under daylight (left) and UV light (365 nm) (right), (g) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra, (h) FL emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths.
Full-range XPS spectrum of carbon steel with 50 mg·L−1 N, S-CDs.
High-resolution XPS spectra of carbon steel with 50 mg·L−1 N, S-CDs for (a) C 1 s, (b) O 1 s, (c) N 1 s, (d) Fe 2p, (e) S 2p.
The SEM images of coupons surface after immersion in solution at (a/b) blank, (c/d/e) 50 mg·L−1 N, S-CDs. (f) is the corresponding EDS data of area in (e).

The surface morphology of carbon steel immersed with 50 mg·L−1 N, S-CDs for (a) 3D AFM image (b) 2D AFM image and (c) corresponding height profile graphs of Line 1 indicated in 2D image for 5 h at 50 °C.
Contact angle measurements on the surface of carbon steel with (a) 0, (b) 50 mg·L−1 4-Aminosalicylic acid, (c) 50 mg·L−1 thiourea, (d) 50 mg·L−1 N, S-CDs.
Time dependence of open circuit potential for carbon steel measured with different concentrations of N, S-CDs at 50 °C.
Polarization curves for carbon steel measured with different concentrations of N, S-CDs at 50 °C.
Nyquist plots for carbon steel with different concentrations of N, S-CDs in different immersion time at 50 °C for (a) blank, (b) 10 mg·L−1, (c) 20 mg·L−1, (d) 30 mg·L−1, (e) 40 mg·L−1, (f) 50 mg·L−1.

Bode and Phase angle plots for carbon steel with different concentrations of N, S-CDs in different immersion time at 50 °C for (a) blank, (b) 10 mg·L−1, (c) 20 mg·L−1, (d) 30 mg·L−1, (e) 40 mg·L−1, (f) 50 mg·L−1.
Electrochemical equivalent circuit for fitting the impedance spectra with (a) Blank (b) N, S-CDs.
Schematic illustration of the inhibition for N, S-CDs at the surface of carbon steel in solution.
Weight loss, corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency of carbon steel with different concentrations of N, S-CDs at 50 °C for 12 h.
Polarization parameters for carbon steel measured with different concentrations of N, S-CDs at 50 °C.
Electrochemical parameters obtained from EIS measurements with different concentrations of N, S-CDs in different immersion time at 50 °C.

head.ce:title: N, S co-doped carbon dots as effective corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel in CO2-saturated 3.5% NaCl solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Materials Chemistry and Service Failure, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Materials Chemistry and Service Failure
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N and S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) were synthesized and used as corrosion inhibitors for the first time in this study. The inhibition performance of N, S-CDs for carbon steel in CO2-saturated NaCl solution was investigated. Results showed that N, S-CDs can effectively protect carbon steel from corrosion and the inhibition efficiency increases as the concentration of N, S-CDs increases, reaching 93% at 50 mg/L. Even at the low concentration, such as 10 mg/L N, S-CDs, the corrosion current density reduces from 1.472 × 10−4 A·cm−2 in the blank condition to 2.99 × 10−5 A·cm−2 after 12 h immersion. N, S-CDs can adsorb on carbon steel through their functional groups and nanoparticles made up of N, S-CDs can form a hydrophobic film on the metal surface, whose thickness was about 40 nm. A theoretical model is established to describe the inhibition mechanism of N, S-CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon steel is extensively used as pipeline material in oil production and transportation because of its high stress strength and cost-effectiveness [1–3]. However, carbon steel is extremely susceptible to corrosion in a CO2-containing environment [2,4]. CO2 is a corrosive species that dissolves in water and generates carbonic acid, which causes early failure of pipelines and results in serious accidents worldwide due to electrochemical corrosion [4–7]. With the development of oil driven by CO2 in oil fields, most of the water produced in oil and gas fields is rich in CO2 [8–10]. The mechanism for CO2 corrosion has been discussed in numerous theories [1,5–7].

Addition of corrosion inhibitors can effectively prevent CO2 corrosion for carbon steel in oil fields. Most corrosion inhibitors are organic reagents containing N, S, and P, which are electronegative and exhibit strong adsorption properties [4,11–13]. The adsorption of these electronegative atoms mainly relies on physicochemical properties, especially for electron density and characteristics of donating electrons in the π orbital, which can form coordination bonds with the outer track of iron [14,15]. At present, numerous CO2 corrosion inhibitors, such as imidazoline derivatives, Schiff bases, and quaternary ammonium salts, have been reported [16,17]. However, the synthesis of these organic compounds is complex and consumes high energy; thus, meeting the requirements of green chemistry becomes difficult [13].

Carbon dots (CDs), which are novel fluorescent nanomaterials, have become increasingly noteworthy because of their high and stable fluorescence, low toxicity, substantial biocompatibility, and stable chemical properties [18–20]. All of these unique and excellent properties make them applicable in biosensing, bioimaging, drug carriers, photocatalysis, and other biological fields [21,22]. To date, numerous researchers have successfully synthesized CDs by laser ablation, microwave radiation, and hydrothermal methods [23–25]. Doping treatment can practically and effectively improve CD properties, such as fluorescence yield, emission spectrum range, and other special features, especially for heteroatom-doped CDs [25,26]. Teng et al. [23] reported a dexterous microwave-assisted method to synthesize N and S co-doped CDs (N, S-CDs) for improving photoluminescence yield. Dinh et al. [26] established a green and cost-efficient solid-state design approach to synthesize N, S-CDs by caffeine that can be utilized sensitively as an Ag+fluorescence probe because of strong fluorescence.

At present, the anti-corrosive applications of carbon nanomaterials, such as super-hydrophobic materials, are mostly devoted to hydrophobic coating [27–29], but few studies have been conducted on carbon nanomaterials functioning as inhibitors in solution. Cui et al. [30] reported the corrosion inhibiting effect of N-doped CDs (N-CDs) in 1 mol·L−1 of HCl and revealed the adsorption pattern of CDs. With the strong electronegativity and electron-donating capability of N and S atoms, N, S-CDs were anticipated as an effective corrosion inhibitor.

In this study, N, S-CDs were synthesized through hydrothermal method using hypotoxic aminosalicylic acid and thiourea and used as corrosion inhibitor. N, S-CDs were characterized by Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, photoluminescence, and elemental analyzer. The inhibition effect of N, S-CDs was investigated using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), potentiodynamic polarization, and weight loss measurement. Surface characterizations of carbon steel were achieved from scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), atomic force microscopy (AFM), and contact angle measurement.

Carbon steel used in this work was composed of (wt.%) 0.34% C, 0.29% Si, 1.39% Mn, 0.015% P, 0.013% S, 0.45% Cr, 0.028% Ni, 0.3% Mo, and balance Fe. Specimens with dimensions of 50 mm × 10 mm × 3 mm were used for weight loss experiments. The cylindrical electrode with an exposed 0.785 cm2 area was used in electrochemical measurements. Prior to tests, the exposed areas were polished with 1200 grit silicon carbide papers, washed with deionized water, degreased with acetone, and finally dried at room temperature.

4-Aminosalicylic acid with 98% purity was purchased from Aladdin, and analytical-grade thiourea was sourced from Sigma–Aldrich. Deionized water and analytical-grade reagent were used to prepare 3.5% NaCl testing solution, which was saturated and aerated by 99.99% CO2 gas for 8 h.

Similar to the procedure reported by Cui et al. [30], the synthesis of N, S-CDs involved hydrothermal method (Fig. 1 ). Mixtures of 1.2 g of aminosalicylic acid and 0.4 g of thiourea were dissolved in 75 mL of ethanol with ultrasonication. Then, the mixtures were transferred into polytetrafluoroethylene autoclaves and heated at 200 °C for 16 h. Dark and transparent products were acquired after natural cooling. The mixtures were filtered through a filter paper, and then the 0.22 μm microporous membrane was used to remove large particles. Thereafter, the solution was purified against deionized water by using a dialysis membrane with 3500 Da pore size for 36 h to detach the impurities. Finally, after solvents were removed from rotary evaporation and further freeze-dried under vacuum, black solids were obtained.

Content of elements was investigated by an elemental analyzer (Elementar, vario Micro Cube, Germany) with error less than 0.1%. The UV–vis results were measured on UV-2600 (Shimadzu, Japan). The photoluminescence emission spectra were recorded by fluorescence spectrophotometry (JASCO, FP-6500, Japan). FTIR spectra were obtained from Nicolet 6700 (Thermo Fisher infrared spectrometer) at wavenumbers of 400–4000 cm−1. The Raman spectrum was acquired by LabRAM HR800 (Horiba JobinYvon). The XRD patterns were characterized using X'Pert PRO (PANalytical B.V., Netherlands), and the TEM images were acquired from FEI Talos F200x (Field Emission Instruments, USA).

A total of three specimens were immersed in CO2-saturated 3.5% NaCl solution at 50 °C for 12 h. The solution was bubbled with CO2 gas continuously throughout experiments. The specimens were weighed thrice by analytical balance before the test. After the experiment, the specimens were extracted and placed into the descaling solution to eliminate corrosion products. Cleaned with deionized water and ethanol in sequence, the specimens were dried by cool air and reweighed accurately to calculate their average corrosion rates. Corrosion rate (vcorr ) can be calculated using the following formula:
(1)
v
c o r r
=
Δ
W
s × t
where ΔW presents the average weight loss (g) of the three specimens, s is the total contact area (m2) between the specimen and medium, and t is the hanging time (h).

Inhibition efficiency (η) for N, S-CDs was calculated by the following formula:
(2)
η =
v
c o r r
0
−
v
c o r r
v
c o r r
0
×
100 %
where vcorr and
v
c o r r
0
are the corrosion rates with and without N, S-CDs, respectively.

The coupons immersed in weight loss experiments were cleaned, dried, and stored in a vacuum drying pot. The morphology of coupon surface was observed by SEM (Phillips Quanta 200 SEM system). The film on the surface of coupons was analyzed by EDS and XPS (AXIS-ULTRA DLD-600 W) with Al Kα excitation source.

To reduce the surface roughness, we ground coupons to mirror face by diamond polishing paste. Then, coupons were immersed in the CO2-saturated 3.5% NaCl solution with 50 mg·L−1 of N, S-CDs at 50 °C for 5 h before contact angle and AFM measurements. Contact angle tester (JC2000, Powereach) was adopted to measure the contact angle of water on carbon steel surface using sessile drop method. After the coupons were hot air-dried, a 2 μL of distilled water droplet was dripped to the coupon surface to obtain the contact angle. The final contact angle value was calculated by the average value of multiple tests in different areas of the coupon. The adsorption of N, S-CDs on the surface of specimen was observed through AFM (SPM-9700, Shimadzu, Japan) under tapping mode.

A Gamry interface 3000 electrochemical workstation was used for electrochemical measurements at 50 °C ± 1 °C. The traditional three-electrode system was adopted in electrochemical testing by using the platinum plane as counter electrode and the saturated calomel electrode as reference electrode. A carbon steel coupon was employed as working electrode, and the device was filled with testing solution, which was bubbled with CO2 continuously throughout the experiments. Prior to electrochemical tests, work electrode should reach a stable state, which means that the open circuit potential (OCP) values should maintain a steady state.
In potentiodynamic polarization tests, scanning was conducted from −100 mV to +200 mV relative to OCP with a sweep rate of 0.5 mV/s.
EIS test was implemented under open-circuit state, and sinusoidal disturbance potential was 5 mV with the frequency ranging from 10 kHz to 10 mHz. EIS data were analyzed by ZView software.

The content (wt.%) of elements in N, S-CDs was investigated by elemental analyzer, comprising 47.05% C, 3.78% H, 10.08% N, and 10.17% S, and surplus element was O. In addition, N and S contents were clearly higher than those in the previous reports [25,30].

The morphology and structure were observed and analyzed by TEM. As revealed in Fig. 2 a, N, S-CDs show uniform spherical structure with the size of 10–20 nm, which is larger than that of the normal CDs due to higher contents of N and S [18,26,31,32]. High-resolution TEM (Fig. 2b) clearly revealed that the synthetic N, S-CDs show noticeable lattice fringes with interplanar distance of around 0.25 nm, showing a high-crystalline structure and in accordance with the reported values of the (102) facet in graphene [26,33].

The XRD pattern (Fig. 2c) presented a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ of approximately 22.7°, with the corresponding interlayer spacing of around 0.39 nm. Hence, the functional groups in N, S-CDs, including O, N, and S groups, led to a broader interlayer space compared with that in graphite (0.34 nm) [21,25] and increased the spacing of carbon layers given the presence of heteroatoms.

The chemical structures of 4-aminosalicylic acid, thiourea, and N, S-CDs were characterized by FTIR. Both spectra of 4-aminosalicylic acid and thiourea were used to contrast absorption peaks with N, S-CDs to confirm the changes in the structures of reactants. As indicated in Fig. 2d, the spectrum for N, S-CDs possessed various absorption bands, the broad absorption peaks at 3364 cm−1 composed by O-H and N-H, and the peaks at around 2925 cm−1 were attributed to the CH bond [21,23,30]. The peak at 2060 cm−1 was typical absorption for cumulated double bonds attributed to N=C=S and N=C=O [34,35] and was unique for N, S-CDs to distinguish from 4-aminosalicylic acid and thiourea. The peak at 1612 cm−1 indicates the characteristic vibration of amide group in stretching, whereas the peaks at 1465 and 1492 cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of C-N and C-S [23,26], respectively. Moreover, the absorption bands of CO bond at 1304 cm−1 and the peak at 1157 cm−1 indicate the existence of –SO3 or C=S [25,30].

For the sake of the analysis on different state of carbon, the Raman spectrum of N, S-CDs (Fig. 2e) was acquired using 532 nm laser, which depicted the two broad peaks at around 1349.6 cm−1 and 1562.8 cm−1, belonging to the D-band and G-band, respectively. The D-band is attributed to sp3- hybridized carbons with dangling bands in disordered structure, while the G-band is associated to sp2- hybridized carbons with the vibration in a 2D hexagonal lattice. The relative intensity ratio between D-band and G-band (I D/I G) is 0.94, indicating the graphitization degree [32,36].

UV–vis and photoluminescence properties were analyzed to understand the optical properties of N, S-CDs. The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 2f) contains two apparent absorption bands. The weak absorption peak at 282 nm was associated to the n–π* electronic transition of C=N bonds, whereas the strong absorption band at 203 nm was assigned to the π–π* electronic transition of sp 2 domains [21,22]. The trapping of excited energy from the passivated surface on heteroatom resulted in the absorption, which may contribute to the strong fluorescence [25,26]. The N, S-CDs exhibited bright green fluorescence (inset in Fig. 2f) when dispersed in ethanol under UV (365 nm). Fig. 2g shows the fluorescence spectrum, indicating that the maximum emission is at 518 nm and excitation is at 470 nm. The emission spectra excited at different wavelengths are exhibited in Fig. 2h, which illustrates the ordinary excitation-dependent emission behaviors of N, S-CDs [23,37]. The photoluminescence emission peak exhibited a red shift from 515 nm to 531 nm as the excitation wavelength increased from 430 nm to 490 nm. An acceptable mechanism [25,38] for the red shift in emission spectra is elucidated from different particle sizes and the complex excited states of N, S-CDs. The high excitation wavelengths may be attributed to large size, and the emissive sites corresponding to definite excitation wavelengths depend on the surface states of N, S-CDs.

The average values of ΔW,vcorr , and η for carbon steel with different concentrations of N, S-CDs are listed in Table 1 . The corrosion rates of carbon steel with the addition of N, S-CDs were markedly smaller than that in blank solution, and the inhibition efficiency generally increases as the concentration of N, S-CDs increased.

The full-range XPS spectrum of the coupon surface after weight loss testing with 50 mg·L−1N, S-CDs is shown in Fig. 3 . The existence of C, N, O, S, and Fe on the coupon surface demonstrated the adsorption of N, S-CDs on carbon steel. Fig. 4 shows the C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, Fe 2p, and S 2p in high-resolution XPS spectra. High-resolution C 1 s spectrum displays three peaks representing the different characteristics of C, including graphitic structure sp 2 C (C=C) at 284.5 eV, sp 3 C (CN, CO) at 285.8 eV, and C=O/C=S at 288.1 eV, as illustrated in Fig. 4a [23,39]. Fig. 4b shows the N 1 s peak, where the peaks at 399.1 and 399.9 eV were assigned to NH and CN groups [21,36], respectively. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4c), can be divided into peaks at 529.9, 531.2, and 532.4 eV, suggesting the presence of Fe2O3, C=O, and CO [30,39], respectively. The Fe 2p 3/2 peaks at 709.7, 710.8, and 712.1 eV (Fig. 4d), are associated with iron in the states of Fe-S, Fe2O3, and FeOOH [40,41], respectively. The appearance of the peak of Fe-S indicates that N, S-CDs form bonds with iron, indicating strong chemisorption of N, S-CDs to carbon steel. The spectrum of S 2p, with peaks curve fitted with three peaks (Fig. 4e), indicating that S atoms clearly appeared in various states. The peaks at 162, 163.8, and 168.3 eV are attributed to S-Fe, C=S/C-S, and –C-S(O) x -C, respectively. The peak of S-Fe was in accordance with the peak of Fe-S in Fe 2p 3/2 spectra. The appearance of –C-S(O) x -C [25,26,42] may be caused by the oxidation of S atoms, illustrating that the synthesized CDs are S-doped CDs.

The surface morphology of coupons after immersion for 12 h without and with 50 mg·L−1N, S-CDs are obtained in Fig. 5 . Carbon steel immersed in blank solution (Fig. 5a, 5b) was highly corroded with deep cracks and ruptured film of corrosion product on the surface. The dense inhibitor film was clearly observed (Fig. 5c, 5d) on the metal surface with the adjunction of N, S-CDs, indicating effective adsorption for N, S-CDs. The micromorphology of N, S-CDs adsorbed in carbon steel was characterized by high-resolution SEM. Dense fine nanoscale particles (Fig. 5d, 5e) revealed the distribution and microstate of N, S-CDs in the carbon steel, with particle size conformed for characterization in TEM. EDS micrograph and composition (Fig. 5f) were analyzed to investigate the composition of particles adsorbed on the surface of carbon steel. The content of S atoms up to 3.84 wt.% clearly demonstrated that the particles in the surface consisted of N, S-CDs. These observations also confirm that N, S-CDs possess favorable adsorptivity and inhibition effect for carbon steel.

AFM is a convenient instrument used for observing the surface morphology microscopically and is preferred to characterize the adsorbate with 3D distribution at metal/solution interface in corrosion processes [43,44]. The 2D AFM image, corresponding to the 3D distributing image and height profile graphs of appointed region in 2D image for carbon steel, which was immersed in solution for 5 h with 50 mg·L−1 of N, S-CDs, were shown in Fig. 6 . The 3D AFM pattern (Fig. 6a) of coupon shows the micromorphology and distribution of N, S-CDs that formed dense film by particles with 141.8 nm maximum height in the viewing area. As shown in height profile graphs of the appointed region (Line 1 Fig. 6b, 6c), most particles ranged from 30 nm to 40 nm in height, with particle size of approximately 30 nm to 60 nm, as observed in SEM. Hence, the inhibitory film constituted by N, S-CDs can completely cover the surface of carbon steel and effectively retard corrosion.

The water contact angle is associated to the wettability of the surface but also considered as a measure to evaluate the hydrophobic property and anti-corrosive performance of inhibitors [15,45]. Fig. 7 shows the water contact angle of coupons immersed in the solution with different conditions at 50 °C for 5 h. As shown in Fig. 7, the contact angle increases from 43° to 92° with the addition of N, S-CDs, while the contact angles for 4-aminosalicylic acid and thiourea under the same experimental condition are 26° and 61°, respectively, indicating that the inhibitor film with strong hydrophobic properties is formed on the metal surface by N, S-CDs. The hydrophobic inhibitory film consisting of carbon nanomaterial can effectively isolate metal surface from corrosive medium, inhibiting the corrosion of carbon steel. Furthermore, the result of contact angle measurements corresponded to the thick film for N, S-CDs observed in SEM.

The evolution of open circuit potential (OCP) with immersion time is essential to evaluate the characters of inhibitor. Fig. 8 shows the time dependence of OCP measured with different concentrations of N, S-CDs for carbon steel. Different from the ordinary inhibitors, the steady-state of N, S-CDs is achieved for much more time, which may be attributed to the slower migration rate of N, S-CDs owing to the larger particle size. The apparent positive shifts relative to the OCP in the blank condition were shown with the presence of N, S-CDs, and the extent of shift is proportional to concentration of N, S-CDs. But the fluctuation time of OCP before reaching a stable value is inversely proportional to concentration of N, S-CDs, i.e., the fluctuation time is minimum when N, S-CDs are added at 50 mg/L, being associated to concentration and diffusion effects. In the initial stage, approximately in first three hours, the values of OCP increase gradually, then reaching a stable state and approaching constant.

The potentiodynamic polarization curves of carbon steel in CO2-saturated 3.5% NaCl solution with and without N, S-CDs are shown in Fig. 9 . Table 2 lists the average values of polarization parameters, including corrosion potential (E corr), corrosion current density (i corr), anodic Tafel slope (b a), and cathodic Tafel slope (b c) deduced from the polarization curves via weak polarization region.

As shown in Fig. 9, E corr positively shifted, in accordance with the evident inhibition of anodic reaction, and icorr decreased considerably with increasing concentration of N, S-CDs. It has been confirmed that the modes for inhibition effect of interface inhibitors were divided into three categories, the geometric blocking effect, the effect of blocking active sites and electro-catalytic effect [46]. The anodic shift of E corr indicates the mode of N, S-CDs was more than the geometric blocking effect definitely, whose shift of E corr would be negligible as the inhibitor being added to the solution [47]. The anodic and cathodic reactions were inhibited apparently compared with those of the blank solution because of the addition of N, S-CDs, indicating that the material is a mixed-type inhibitor preventing the anodic reaction more evidently. This result can be associated with chemisorption between the iron and inhibitor, which may be attributed to the active sites blocking effect, not only preventing the dissolution of carbon steel but also depressing the cathodic evolution reaction [15,30]. As shown in the curves, the diffusion-controlled cathodic process was apparent in the blank condition, while it disappeared and corrosion current density decrease with the addition of N, S-CDs, indicating the charge transfer processes were inhibited. Besides, the similar platform areas where anode current increases significant arise obviously in the anodic curves, when the polarization potential reaches relatively positive with the presence of N, S-CDs, approximately in the −0.53 V. Once exceeding this polarization potential, anodic polarization curves turn into coincidence or proximity coincidence with curves in blank condition, which is attributed to the rapidly decrease of N, S-CDs adsorbed on the metal surface. With the increase of anodic polarization, not only the anodic dissolution rate of the metal surface exposed to solution increases, but also the dissolution process of the carbon steel covered by the adsorbed N, S-CDs will occur at a higher rate when the anodic polarization is large enough. The desorption of N, S-CDs is associated with both the thermal motion and the anodic dissolution of iron atoms covered by them, which is described as anodic desorption of inhibitors at desorption potential, E des, and so that the coverage of N, S-CDs on metal surface decreases rapidly, resulting in the current density of anodic dissolution of carbon steel increases significantly [48]. Furthermore, the platform region is wider and the E des is higher with increasing the concentration of N, S-CDs, which is attributed to the more adherent inhibitor film on the metal surface [49].

Figs. 10 and 11 illustrate the Nyquist plots and Bode plots for the coupons measured in the absence and presence of inhibitors with different concentrations in various test time, respectively. The electrochemical impedance spectra were performed continuously as a function of time to determine a stable corrosion rate and investigate the performance change of corrosion for carbon steel at different time. The diameter of the capacitive semicircle remarkably increased with the addition of N, S-CDs, indicating that N, S-CDs formed an effective film on carbon steel. EIS in the blank solution (Fig. 10(a)) was exhibited by two capacitive loops and one inductive loop distributing in high, low, and intermediate frequencies, respectively. In the high-frequency range, the capacitive loop was determined by double-layer capacitance, whereas the other capacitive loop at low frequency was assigned to membrane resistance of corrosion products. The formation of inductive loop tends to be associated with the adsorption of corrosion products, such as intermediate [(FeOH)ads] [50]. The constant inductive loops show the sustained active dissolution of the steel surface and the unprotected scales, which have local defects and failures such as pores [6]. Besides that, the diameter of capacitive semicircle decrease gradually with increasing the immersion time, which is associated with the preferential dissolution of ferrite [51,52]. Then, the change of curves is diminished after 3 h, indicating the realization of stable corrosion rate. Once adding the N, S-CDs, the diameter of the capacitive semicircle is considerably enlarged as the concentration of N, S-CDs increased, showing enhanced inhibition efficiency for protecting carbon steel. The flat capacitive arc was mainly related to the adsorption of N, S-CDs, with the formation of a compact overlayer film composed of particles, Fe ion only penetrating the film in the local area forming the anode current [41,47]. The coefficients of dispersion effect were reduced with the current maldistribution to flatten the capacitive arc. As shown in curves (Fig. 10 (b–f)), the amplification of capacitive semicircle is obvious in first three hours and maintains stable after six hours, which is corresponding with the variety of OCP. The more N, S-CDs were added, the smaller differences were measured in different immersion time. In the Bode phase angle plots (Fig. 11 (b–f)), it can be seen that only one time constant emerges at intermediate frequencies, which is associated with electric double-layer capacitance. Remarkably, Bode phase angle plots show the wider signal in the high concentration of N, S-CDs, just as two time constants overlap, indicating the compact inhibitor film with less porous. Furthermore, the maximum phase angle increases with the increase of both the immersion time and the concentration of N, S-CDs, in correspondence with inhibition mechanism that N, S-CDs only block part of the active sites on the metal surface and the compact film whose pore size is small hinder the transmission of corrosive species [48].

To better fit the parameters in impedance spectra, we proposed two equivalent circuits to analyze the EIS data (Fig. 12 ), which were measured both at different concentrations of N, S-CDs and immersion time. As for equivalent circuits, R s is solution resistance, CPE ct and CPE f are the constant phase elements, representing the double-layer capacitance and the capacitance of scale on the metal surface, respectively. R pore is the resistance for film of corrosion products, which is controlled by the penetration of ions through the scale, R ct represents charge transfer resistance, R L is inductance resistance, and L is inductance. The electrochemical parameters obtained from fitting by equivalent circuits are shown in Table 3 . In the blank condition, both the R pore and R ct decrease gradually with increasing the immersion time, indicating the poor protective scale and accelerated corrosion rate, while the CPE ct and CPE f increase with the immersion time, which is attributed to the more roughness of scale and metal surface. But the CPE ct reduced significantly with the presence of N, S-CDs in contrast to that in blank condition and decreased by degrees with increasing the concentration of N, S-CDs. The N, S-CDs need remove the water previously absorbed on the metal surface before they are adsorbed to carbon steel. Owing to the fact that the dielectric constant of water is much bigger than that of N, S-CDs and the thickness of the adsorbed N, S-CDs layer is very big, hence the capacitance of the interfacial layer composed of N, S-CDs is obviously smaller [53,54]. Moreover, R ct increase distinctly with the addition of N, S-CDs and the increment is proportional to the concentration of N, S-CDs, indicating the effective inhibition film and prominent inhibition effect. Above the analyses show that the effects of both geometric blocking and active sites blocking are reflected for N, S-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstate of N, S-CDs on the metal surface
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic N, S-CDs can be adsorbed on the metal surface to form a compact protective film. The uniform spherical structure of synthetic N, S-CDs within 20 nm in diameter was observed through TEM, which is consistent with the surveys in SEM and AFM. After being immersed in solution with N, S-CDs, coupons were covered by the film, which consisted of fine nanoparticles. The process of adsorption is slower relatively to the conventional molecule via the analysis of OCP variety and most of the particles were accumulated, as observed in SEM. Up to 3.84 wt.% of S atoms in EDS for particles indicated that the nanoparticles were mainly the conglomeration of N, S-CDs. Nanoparticles are easily reunited by interaction and solid-state reaction with intermolecular force (including hydrogen bonds) and electrostatic interaction due to the small size effect and surface effect of the nanoparticles [55,56]. The 3D image in AFM displayed the stereoscopic morphology of nanoparticles with the maximum height of multilayer stacking at around 140 nm. Moreover, a large portion of coupons shows the height ranges from 30 nm to 40 nm in the height profile graphs, indicating that N, S-CDs may accumulate three or four layers. Consequently, the microstate of N, S-CDs in solution can be described in the following process. First, a small amount of N, S-CDs approached to the metal surface through diffusion and adsorbed in superficies. Then, the N, S-CDs near the interface were diffused to the nanoparticles in the superficies due to surface energy and conglomerate to remain stable. Thereafter, the concentration difference and effect of the reunion of nanoparticles promote N, S-CDs in the solution continuously diffusing to metal surface, stacking particles together, and finally becoming the film covered on the surface.

In summary, N, S-CDs were synthesized as new corrosion inhibitors. Comprehensive measurements were adopted to investigate the inhibition efficiency and mechanism of N, S-CDs in CO2-saturated 3.5% NaCl solution at 50 °C. The results are listed as follows:
(1)
N, S-CDs can effectively protect carbon steel from corrosion, with inhibition efficiency reaching 93% at 50 mg·L−1.
(2)
Morphological characterizations for N, S-CDs at the surface of carbon steel by SEM and AFM indicate that coupons were covered by film consisting of nanoparticles, which were the agglomeration of N, S-CDs.
(3)
Functional groups on the surface of N, S-CDs can adhere to the superficies by chemisorption with Fe.
(4)
Nanoparticles in N, S-CDs can make up a hydrophobic film because of the agglomeration effect and covered metal surface, which prevent direct contact with corrosion medium.
The authors declare that they have no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51571098) and the analytical support of the Analytical and Testing Center, Huazhong University of Science and Technology.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2019.02.036.
